agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO describes it at a high level with explicit acknowledgment that guardrails are needed but undefined. This is an early-stage product concept with significant ambiguity.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The product name for the real-time dynamic delivery pricing system" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of delivery request volume relative to capacity in a given area/time window — exact definition unspecified" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing — thresholds undefined" },
      { "term": "Base Price", "definition": "Implied but unstated: the standard delivery price before dynamic adjustment is applied" },
      { "term": "Price Adjustment", "definition": "The delta (positive or negative) applied to base price in response to demand signals" },
      { "term": "Real-time", "definition": "Pricing reflects current demand conditions at the moment of quote — exact latency tolerance unspecified" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must be adjusted based on current demand levels — higher demand produces higher prices, lower demand produces lower prices.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the core pricing policy. A VP could change the pricing strategy (e.g., switch to cost-plus, flat rate, or time-of-day pricing). It is a business policy, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand level D1 > D2 in the same zone and time window, the price quoted at D1 must be greater than the price quoted at D2, all else being equal."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur in real time — the quoted price must reflect demand conditions at the time of the quote, not stale or batch-computed data.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Real-time is a latency policy. The business could decide hourly recalculation is acceptable. This is a performance/freshness policy, not a state integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "The demand signal used to compute a delivery price must have been sampled within a configurable freshness window (exact SLA undefined — suggest ≤ 60 seconds as starting point)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "The system must be fully automated — no manual human approval is required for individual price adjustments.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation level is an operational policy. The business could introduce manual approval for certain thresholds or edge cases. A VP could change this.",
      "testable_assertion": "Price adjustments are applied without any human-in-the-loop step between demand signal ingestion and price quote delivery."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Price guardrails must exist to prevent prices from reaching extreme values (both excessively high and excessively low).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without guardrails, the system could produce zero or negative prices (destroying revenue) or predatory/illegal prices (regulatory and reputational risk). The existence of bounds is a system integrity constraint — without them the system is corrupt. The specific thresholds are policy, but the presence of bounds is invariant.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every quoted price P must satisfy: configured_minimum_price ≤ P ≤ configured_maximum_price. No price may ever be quoted outside these bounds."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "A delivery price must never be zero or negative.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative delivery price means the company pays the customer to deliver — this violates basic financial integrity. Even in lowest demand, the price must cover some floor. If this is violated, the system is producing corrupt output.",
      "testable_assertion": "For all quoted prices P: P > 0."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The pricing function must be monotonically non-decreasing with respect to demand — price must not decrease as demand increases.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If higher demand produced lower prices, the system would exhibit incoherent economic behavior, incentivize demand gaming, and produce irrational outcomes. This is a logical consistency constraint on the pricing function itself.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any two demand levels where D_high > D_low, with all other inputs equal: price(D_high) ≥ price(D_low)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The system must balance competitiveness and profitability — prices must not be so high they destroy demand, nor so low they destroy margin.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a strategic business objective, not a hard constraint. The balance point is a policy decision that will shift based on market conditions, competitive landscape, and business strategy.",
      "testable_assertion": "NOT directly testable as stated — requires decomposition into specific margin floors and price ceiling policies relative to competitor benchmarks. This rule needs refinement."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "A price quoted to a customer must be honored for that transaction — the price cannot change between quote and order confirmation.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Implied by any transactional pricing system. If a customer sees price X and is charged price Y, this is a data integrity violation and potentially illegal (bait-and-switch). The quoted price must be locked for a validity window.",
      "testable_assertion": "Once a delivery price is quoted for a specific order, the charged amount must equal the quoted amount, provided the customer confirms within the quote validity window."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes instantaneously (e.g., concert ending, natural disaster)", "expected_behavior": "Guardrail ceiling must cap price. Rate-of-change limiting may also be needed to prevent price shock. Regulatory scrutiny risk in disaster scenarios (price gouging laws)." },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to near zero (e.g., 3 AM in low-density area)", "expected_behavior": "Guardrail floor must prevent price from going below minimum viable price. System must still quote a valid positive price." },
    { "scenario": "Demand signal source fails or becomes stale", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Should the system fall back to base price? Refuse to quote? Use last known demand? This is a critical gap." },
    { "scenario": "Two customers request quotes simultaneously in the same zone with identical parameters", "expected_behavior": "Both must receive the same price — the pricing function must be deterministic for identical inputs at the same demand level." },
    { "scenario": "Customer receives a quote, demand changes, customer confirms order", "expected_behavior": "Original quoted price must be honored within validity window (per BR-8). System must not retroactively adjust." },
    { "scenario": "Competitor prices are significantly lower during high demand", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. The spec says 'competitive' but provides no mechanism for competitor-aware pricing. Risk of pricing yourself out of the market." },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price gouging restrictions apply (e.g., during declared emergencies)", "expected_behavior": "Undefined and high risk. Dynamic pricing during emergencies may violate local price gouging laws. Guardrails alone may be insufficient — may need emergency override to disable surge pricing entirely." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Potential revenue uplift during high-demand periods through surge pricing. Risk of revenue loss if guardrails are misconfigured (too low ceiling caps revenue, too high floor destroys volume). Margin improvement if low-demand discounting drives incremental volume that wouldn't exist at base price.",
    "operational": "Requires real-time demand sensing infrastructure, pricing engine with sub-second response times, and monitoring/alerting for guardrail breaches. Introduces operational complexity: someone must own guardrail thresholds, monitor pricing health, and respond to anomalies.",
    "user": "Customers will experience variable pricing — requires clear communication to avoid trust erosion. Price transparency and fairness perception are critical. Customers may game the system by waiting for low-demand windows.",
    "risk": "HIGH — regulatory risk from price gouging laws in disaster/emergency scenarios. Reputational risk from perceived unfair pricing. Technical risk from demand signal failures causing incorrect pricing. Competitive risk if surge prices consistently exceed market rates.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Will pay variable prices; may perceive unfairness during surge periods. Need transparency on why prices vary." },
      { "group": "Delivery Drivers/Couriers", "impact": "Higher demand pricing may correlate with higher pay during surges — or may not. Relationship between dynamic customer pricing and driver compensation is unspecified." },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue Team", "impact": "Must model revenue under dynamic pricing; need new reporting and forecasting capabilities." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about surge pricing. Need scripts and policies for price disputes." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review pricing against price gouging laws, consumer protection regulations, and contractual obligations per jurisdiction." },
      { "group": "Operations/Dispatch", "impact": "Pricing signals may affect demand distribution and thus capacity planning." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "The CEO's description is a high-level vision statement, not a specification. Critical parameters are undefined: demand measurement method, guardrail thresholds, price floor/ceiling values, quote validity windows, fallback behavior, competitive benchmarking mechanism, and regulatory compliance approach. The phrase 'probably have some guardrails' indicates even the guardrail requirement is tentative. Multiple business-critical decisions remain open. This is insufficient to build against.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Prices adjust upward when demand is high (BR-1)",
      "Prices adjust downward when demand is low (BR-1)",
      "System is automated without manual approval per transaction (BR-3)",
      "Some form of guardrails will exist (BR-4)",
      "Prices must be positive (BR-5)"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "How demand is measured (volume? capacity ratio? geographic granularity?)",
      "What the guardrail thresholds are (min/max price, max rate of change)",
      "Quote validity window duration",
      "Fallback behavior when demand signals are unavailable",
      "Whether competitor pricing is an input to the algorithm",
      "Regulatory compliance strategy per jurisdiction",
      "Relationship between customer pricing and driver/courier compensation",
      "What 'real-time' means in latency terms"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A base price exists that is adjusted up or down — the CEO did not explicitly state this but it is implied",
      "BR-8 (price quote honoring) is assumed as a standard transactional integrity requirement though never mentioned",
      "BR-5 (positive price) is assumed as basic financial sanity though never stated",
      "BR-6 (monotonic pricing) is assumed from the directional language ('high demand = high price') but the exact functional form is unknown",
      "Demand is measured per geographic zone and time window — granularity unspecified"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Demand measurement is completely undefined. Is demand measured as absolute request volume, requests per available courier, zone-level saturation, or something else? The pricing function cannot be built without this.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Data Science", "Operations"],
      "options": ["A) Request volume per zone per time window", "B) Request-to-courier ratio (capacity utilization)", "C) Composite signal including volume, capacity, and historical patterns"],
      "recommendation": "Option B — capacity utilization ratio is the standard approach in logistics surge pricing as it directly encodes supply/demand imbalance."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Guardrail thresholds are undefined. The CEO said 'probably' have guardrails and 'so prices don't go crazy' — no specific bounds, no rate-of-change limits, no emergency overrides.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance", "Legal/Compliance", "Customers"],
      "options": ["A) Fixed min/max multipliers on base price (e.g., 0.5x to 3.0x)", "B) Configurable per-zone, per-time thresholds", "C) Combination of absolute bounds + rate-of-change limits"],
      "recommendation": "Option C — absolute bounds prevent extreme prices while rate-of-change limits prevent price shock. Both are needed."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No regulatory analysis has been mentioned. Dynamic pricing in logistics/delivery may be subject to price gouging laws during emergencies in many jurisdictions. This is a legal compliance risk that must be addressed before launch.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Executive Team", "Customers"],
      "options": ["A) Engage legal review before defining guardrails", "B) Build emergency override kill-switch that disables surge pricing per jurisdiction", "C) Both A and B"],
      "recommendation": "Option C — legal review is mandatory, and a kill-switch is a technical safety net regardless."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The relationship between 'competitive' and 'profitable' is undefined. These goals inherently conflict — no mechanism for resolving the tension is specified (e.g., target margin floor, competitor price ceiling, market-rate indexing).",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance", "Strategy", "Sales"],
      "options": ["A) Define a minimum margin floor that takes precedence over competitiveness", "B) Define a competitor price ceiling that caps surge pricing relative to market", "C) Define both with configurable priority"],
      "recommendation": "Option A — profitability floor should be the hard constraint; competitiveness is optimized within that constraint."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Quote validity window is unspecified. How long does a quoted dynamic price remain valid? Without this, BR-8 (price honoring) cannot be implemented. Too short frustrates customers; too long exposes the company to stale pricing risk.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Customers", "Finance"],
      "options": ["A) Short validity (30-60 seconds)", "B) Medium validity (5-15 minutes)", "C) Configurable per order type"],
      "recommendation": "Option B — 5-15 minutes is standard in ride-hailing/delivery. Short enough to reflect demand, long enough for customer decision."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
